results.possibleIdentity: Michael Kirby (1931–1997) was a prominent American artist, theorist, and academic who was a central figure in the New York avant-garde scene during the 1960s and 1970s. A multi-disciplinary creator, Kirby was active as a sculptor, performance artist, and filmmaker, but he is perhaps most recognized as a leading chronicler and theorist of the "Happenings" movement. He authored the seminal 1965 book "Happenings," which provided the first detailed documentation and theoretical framework for the genre, and followed it with "The Art of Time: Essays on the Avant-Garde" in 1969. Kirby was deeply involved with the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) circle; he was a performer in the landmark 1966 event "9 Evenings: Theatre and Engineering," appearing in Yvonne Rainer's "Carriage Discreteness" and Steve Paxton's "Physical Things." Professionally, he was a long-time professor at New York University (NYU), where he was a driving force in the creation of the Performance Studies department and served as the influential editor of "TDR: The Drama Review" from 1970 to 1985. His work focused on "non-matrixed" performance, a concept he developed to describe acting that avoided traditional characterization and plot in favor of direct, task-based activity.

results.possibleIdentity: Juan Downey (1940–1993) was a Chilean-born artist, architect, and a seminal figure in the development of video and interactive art. After receiving a degree in architecture in Chile and studying printmaking in Paris, he moved to the United States in 1965, eventually settling in New York City in 1969. During the late 1960s and early 1970s, Downey was a key participant in the emerging movement that combined art, science, and technology. He was associated with the organization Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) and participated in their landmark 1968 exhibition, 'Some More Beginnings,' at the Brooklyn Museum. His early work included 'Audio-Kinetic Electronic Sculptures,' created in collaboration with engineer Fred Pitts, and interactive projects like 'Do It Yourself: The Human Voice' (1967) and 'Do Your Own Concert' (1968), which were influenced by cybernetics and feedback systems. Downey's most influential work, the 'Video Trans Americas' series (1973–1976), merged autobiography and anthropology as he traveled through North and South America documenting indigenous cultures. He served as a fellow at MIT's Center for Advanced Visual Studies (CAVS) in 1973 and 1975 and was a professor at the Pratt Institute for over two decades.

He was associated with the organization Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) and participated in their landmark 1968 exhibition, 'Some More Beginnings,' at the Brooklyn Museum.
